findings.category: O(n*m) repeated linear scan in hot path
findings.title: Runner.getMethods() invoked per stock in trend loop and again in pass-count loop
findings.details: Inside evaluateAllStocks() the trends IIFE runs `for (const m of Runner.getMethods())` per stock (line 59) and a second `for (const m of Runner.getMethods())` runs for passCount per stock (line 69). getMethods() rebuilds an array via .filter().filter().map() over METHODS every call. For 4000 stocks × ~25 methods this is 4000 array reconstructions plus 4000×25 type lookups (MT.getType, MT.isDefaultActive).
findings.impact: Wasted CPU; cache `Runner.getMethods()` once before the loop and reuse `methods` reference. Estimated >50% reduction in evaluateAllStocks time on 4000-stock universe.
findings.fix: Hoist `const _methods = Runner.getMethods();` above the file loop and pass it into the trends IIFE and pass-count loop.

findings.category: O(rows × methods) repeated full-array scans
findings.title: Method summary recomputes rows.filter() twice per method
findings.details: renderHTML's method summary (line 347-351) and main()'s console output (line 1052-1055) iterate `for (const m of methods)` and inside each iteration call `rows.filter(r => r.results[m.id].computable).length` and `rows.filter(r => r.results[m.id].pass).length`. With ~25 methods and 4000 rows this is 25 × 2 × 4000 = 200k iterations purely to compute counts that could be tallied in a single O(rows × methods) pass.
findings.impact: Doubles the cost of summary generation; ~200k unnecessary scans per render. Also creates two intermediate arrays per filter, adding GC pressure.
findings.fix: Single pass: `const counts = {}; for (const r of rows) for (const m of methods) { counts[m.id] = counts[m.id] || {c:0,p:0}; if (r.results[m.id].computable) counts[m.id].c++; if (r.results[m.id].pass) counts[m.id].p++; }`. Same precomputed map is then used by both summary HTML and stdout.

findings.category: O(rows × methods × methods) linear search
findings.title: Runner.METHODS.find(m => m.id === ...) inside per-row render loops
findings.details: renderCard (line 337) and renderRow (line 417) call `Runner.METHODS.find(m => m.id === mid)` for every chip in every card's scoreBreakdown. With ~25 methods, ~8 chips per card, and up to ~200 cards per mode × 3 modes = 4800 .find() calls, each doing a linear scan of METHODS. Also topAllMust() (line 228) does the lookup once per render which is fine, but the per-card path is O(rows × chips × methods).
findings.impact: ~120k array scans for full 200-pick × 3-mode render. CPU-bound, not user-blocking but wasted.
findings.fix: Build a single methodById map once at module scope: `const METHOD_BY_ID = new Map(Runner.METHODS.map(m => [m.id, m]));` then `METHOD_BY_ID.get(mid)` is O(1).

findings.category: Serial fs.readFileSync inside loop over full universe
findings.title: Three separate scripts each serial-read 4000+ snapshot files via fs.readFileSync
findings.details: Each of these scripts iterates fs.readdirSync(snapshots) and does fs.readFileSync(path.join(dir, f)) + JSON.parse synchronously in a tight loop, blocking the event loop. snapshot-methods-history.js has already adopted async batching (F-PF-007 in code, line 23-40), but generate-methods-report.js, generate-modes-report.js, detect-changes.js and sector-medians-compute.js have not. On a 4000-file universe this is 4 × 4000 = 16k blocking syscalls per CI run.
findings.impact: Each script adds ~5-15s of pure I/O wait time. With 4 scripts → ~20-60s of serialized I/O that could be parallelized in batches of 200 (as snapshot-methods-history.js demonstrates).
findings.fix: Adopt the same loadFilesAsync(dir, fileList) pattern already present in snapshot-methods-history.js (lines 24-40). Read in async batches of 200 via Promise.all. Or extract a shared helper `loadSnapshotsAsync` used by all four call sites.

findings.category: Redundant JSON.stringify with pretty-print on large output
findings.title: JSON.stringify(obj, null, 2) used for snapshot files written per-ticker
findings.details: pull-yahoo.js writes each snapshot via `JSON.stringify(canonical, null, 2)` (line 853) — for a 4000-ticker pull that means 4000 pretty-print serializations and ~30-40% larger files on disk. sector-medians-compute.js does the same for medians files (smaller, OK there). The price-only update path (line 680) and delisted-flag write (line 897) also use indent-2.
findings.impact: ~30% larger snapshot files (4000 × ~15KB indent overhead ≈ 60MB extra disk per pull). Slower JSON.parse downstream (every script that reads snapshots). Slower fs.writeFile.
findings.fix: Use `JSON.stringify(canonical)` for snapshot files (machine-only). snapshot-methods-history.js already does this (line 123). Keep indent only for human-edited files (sector-medians.json, watchlist.json, _manifest.json maybe). Note that some downstream tooling may parse snapshots — they will still parse correctly without indents.

findings.category: Repeated JSON.parse of every snapshot across the pipeline
findings.lines: pull-yahoo.js (writes); detect-changes.js:235; generate-methods-report.js:43; generate-modes-report.js:74; snapshot-methods-history.js:31; sector-medians-compute.js:252
findings.title: Five pipeline stages each JSON.parse the entire snapshot universe independently
findings.details: In one CI run, every snapshot file is re-read and re-parsed five times: detect-changes, generate-methods-report, generate-modes-report, snapshot-methods-history, sector-medians-compute. For 4000 × ~30KB snapshots and ~25 methods each, that is roughly 5 × 4000 × 30KB = 600MB of I/O and 5 full JSON.parse passes (≈ 5-25s wall-clock each on warm OS cache).
findings.impact: Wasted serial work in CI. Combined wall-clock could be reduced 60-80% by parsing each snapshot once into a shared in-memory store.
findings.fix: Introduce a pipeline-level wrapper that loads all snapshots once into an in-memory object, then invokes detect/methods/modes/history/medians in-process. Or write a single 'all-stocks.bin' (msgpack/cbor) or a packed JSON array once after pull, and have downstream scripts read that single file. msgpack would also remove the pretty-print parse cost.

findings.category: evaluateStock allocates a new methodType lookup per (stock, method) pair
findings.title: MT.getType / wrapEvaluate per-call overhead in tight 4000×25 loop
findings.details: evaluateStock loops over METHODS per stock and calls `MT.getType(m.id)` (line 49), `MT.isDefaultActive(m.id)` (line 51), then `H.wrapEvaluate(m, stock, {methodType})` (line 52). wrapEvaluate then runs `_dataAsOfFromStock` (rebuilds a 4-element array per call), `_dataAgeDays`, `_inferConfidence`, `_autoFlags`, and `Object.assign({}, raw, {...})` allocations for every (stock, method) pair. That is 4000 × 25 = 100k of these allocations per pipeline run, run multiple times across pipeline stages.
findings.impact: GC pressure and CPU. The methodType, asOf, ageDays are all stock-level — same for all methods of a given stock — but recomputed per method.
findings.fix: (a) Cache methodType per id in a Map at module load. (b) In evaluateStock(), compute dataAsOf + ageDays once per stock and pass into wrapEvaluate via opts so wrapEvaluate skips the recomputation. (c) Return a Result class instance instead of Object.assign({}, raw, {...}) to avoid the spread allocation.

findings.category: Per-iteration regex compilation
findings.title: Regex literals inside processOne / quoteSummaryWithRetry hot loop
findings.details: Inside processOne (called per ticker), `/purchase/i`, `/automatic|option|grant|award|vest|exercise/i`, `/sale|sell/i` etc. are evaluated against each insider transaction; inside quoteSummaryWithRetry the rate-limit detection runs `/429|too many request|rate.?limit/i.test(...)` per failure. Inside the catch block (line 879-883), five regexes are tested per failure. These are recompiled on each call (JS engines do cache simple literals, but the regex objects are still re-evaluated as patterns each call).
findings.impact: Minor — modern V8 caches RegExp literals. Mentioned for completeness; promote to module scope for clarity and a small consistency win.
findings.fix: Hoist regexes to module scope: `const RX_INSIDER_BUY = /purchase/i; const RX_INSIDER_EXCLUDE = /automatic|option|grant|award|vest|exercise/i; const RX_RATE_LIMIT = /429|too many request|rate.?limit/i; const RX_NOT_FOUND = /...`. Same as F-PF-011 already done for COMPANY_SUFFIX_REGEX in modes-report.

findings.category: Promise.all without concurrency limit in async-batch helper
findings.title: loadFilesAsync batch of 200 spawns 200 simultaneous fs.readFileSync calls
findings.details: Each batch in loadFilesAsync calls Promise.all on 200 entries, but inside each entry it calls `fs.readFileSync` (the sync version, wrapped in a Promise via Promise.all/.map). The sync call blocks the event loop sequentially even though it sits inside Promise.all — so 'BATCH=200' provides no actual parallelism. The .map executes synchronously, each readFileSync runs serially, then Promise.all just resolves the already-completed values.
findings.impact: Marketed as a performance optimization but is effectively still serial. The pattern silently lies about being async-batched.
findings.fix: Replace fs.readFileSync with `fs.promises.readFile` (or `await fs.readFile(... , 'utf8')`) so the I/O runs truly in parallel. Then BATCH=200 will give 20-50× speedup over serial.

findings.category: Unbounded parallel Yahoo I/O concurrency
findings.title: PULL_CONCURRENCY used twice: yahoo-finance2 queue + worker pool
findings.details: Line 57-58 instantiates yahoo-finance2 with `queue: { concurrency: PULL_CONCURRENCY }` AND lines 917-931 spin PULL_CONCURRENCY workers each making yf.quoteSummary + yf.fundamentalsTimeSeries calls. The library queue and the outer worker pool both throttle concurrency, so effective parallelism is min(library_queue, workers). If workers=10 but each ticker makes 6 yf calls (1 quoteSummary + 4 FTS + 1 quote), the lib queue gates at 10 — workers wait on queue. Worse: if PULL_CONCURRENCY=20, library queue=20, but actual TCP/HTTP concurrency is unclear. Composing two concurrency limits doubles the configuration surface and creates head-of-line blocking.
findings.impact: Throughput is unpredictable. A spike in rate-limit retries inside the library queue blocks all workers. Quoted 'with 20 workers... ~13 tickers/sec' (line 914-915) may be optimistic if queue gates the per-call concurrency.
findings.fix: Either set library queue to Infinity and rely solely on the worker pool, or remove the worker pool and rely on yahoo-finance2's queue. Best practice: one concurrency limit per pool. Document choice in code.

findings.category: Synchronous file ops in worker pool inner loop
findings.title: fs.readFileSync / writeFileSync inside per-ticker hot path
findings.details: Every processOne call does at least: sortByStaleness (per-ticker open+read, already called once outside loop — OK), _getExistingSnapshotAge (open + read 500 bytes per ticker, called inside processOne — fs.openSync/readSync/closeSync), _priceOnlyUpdate (full fs.readFileSync of existing snapshot + JSON.parse + fs.writeFileSync), full-pull writes (fs.writeFileSync of new snapshot), cache writes (fs.writeFileSync of fundamentals-cache). Each of these is a blocking syscall on the event loop. With 20 concurrent workers, syscalls serialize on the libuv thread pool (default 4 threads) — the apparent concurrency = min(4, workers).
findings.impact: I/O wait blocks all workers; defeats the worker-pool concurrency benefit during peak file activity. Effective throughput is gated by sync I/O, not network.
findings.fix: Use fs.promises (fs.promises.readFile/writeFile/stat) inside processOne. Workers will then actually overlap their I/O. UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE can also be raised in package.json/start script if going further.

findings.category: Linear scan instead of hash map lookup
findings.title: gatherBySubProfile iterates stocks but classify() may itself iterate sub-profile rules per stock
findings.details: Inside the stocks loop (line 37-78), `classify(stock)` is called once per stock (good), but the lookup chain inside is delegated to engine-v7.3.js which (depending on impl) does a linear walk of sub-profile rules. Not directly a bug here, but the doubled bucket-push (both globalBuckets AND regionalBuckets[region][sp.id], lines 50-77) duplicates all four metric extractions (ni/ta/cash/oi/fcf/mc), doubling per-stock work for every (region, subProfile) update. The metrics are extracted once but pushed twice with re-computed `ic = ta - (cash || 0)` etc.
findings.impact: ~2× the bucket-bookkeeping for 4000 stocks. Minor at this size but trivially fixable.
findings.fix: Compute the three derived metrics once: `const _roic = (ni != null && ic > 0) ? ni/ic : null;` etc. Then push the precomputed value into both buckets via a small helper.

findings.category: Memory growth — full results in memory for 4000 stocks × 25 methods
findings.title: All stocks + all results held in RAM simultaneously
findings.details: loadFilesAsync returns the full loaded array (snapshot-methods-history.js:77), then iterates. generate-modes-report.js loadStocks() (line 70) returns the full stocks array, then evaluateAll() builds another full array of {stock, allResults, mcap, ipoYear, modeEvals}. For 4000 stocks each with ~30KB snapshot + 25-method results + 3-mode modeEvals (which embed scoreBreakdown per method), peak RAM is roughly 4000 × ~80KB = ~300MB. GitHub Actions default runner has 7GB so it fits, but the entire pipeline is held in RAM with no streaming.
findings.impact: Larger universes (>10k tickers, planned per file comments) would exceed runner memory. Also slows GC cycles in the V8 heap.
findings.fix: Stream per-stock: read snapshot → evaluate → write to per-stock output → drop reference. Currently buildHtml needs the whole array, so the report-generation step would still need them all in memory. But snapshot-methods-history (which only needs to write to disk) could stream and free each stock object as soon as it's processed.

findings.category: Repeated computation of sub-profile / data-quality grade
findings.title: classifySubProfile called per method per stock, and again inside effectiveThreshold
findings.details: effectiveThreshold() runs `classifySubProfile(stock)` at line 140 — and it's called from every method that uses dynamic thresholds (e.g. ROIC, FCF yield), so for 25 methods × 4000 stocks the sub-profile is reclassified ~100k times. classifySubProfile delegates to Engine.classifySubProfile which does its own rule-based walk. Even though `_subProfileCache` is module-level, it only caches the engine reference, not the per-stock classification result.
findings.impact: Wasted CPU. Single ticker's sub-profile is stable across methods within a single evaluation pass, but is reclassified every time.
findings.fix: Add a WeakMap<stock, subProfile> cache, or compute the sub-profile once in evaluateStock() (runner.js) and pass it via opts to wrapEvaluate, which forwards into effectiveThreshold via the stock or a context object.

findings.category: String concatenation in HTML render loop
findings.title: Method-cell tableRows built via repeated string concatenation
findings.details: tableRows builder (line 318) maps rows.map → for each row methodCells.map → returns a template literal concatenation. For 4000 stocks × 25 method cells = 100k template-literal evaluations producing a string. The .join('') at the end is fine, but the intermediate per-row template literals do per-row string growth. F-PF-013 already addressed sparkline string concat in modes-report (line 258-263), but this larger one in the matrix table HTML remains.
findings.impact: Minor — V8 optimizes template literals well. Mention because the same pattern was flagged in modes-report and the same convention should apply.
findings.fix: Already acceptable. If needed: collect parts into a chunks array and join at the end. Lower priority.

findings.category: Repeated work — _arrVals re-allocates per-sparkline
findings.title: buildSparkline runs _arrVals on every renderCard call
findings.details: Each card renders calls buildSparkline(s), which calls `_arrVals(a)` — a `.map().filter()` over annual.annualRev for each card. With ~600 cards rendered per build (200 picks × 3 modes), that's 600 × map+filter passes. Not catastrophic, but the values could be precomputed per stock once in evaluateAll().
findings.impact: Minor CPU; visible in profiles when stack depth gets to renderCard.
findings.fix: Precompute `ev._sparkVals = _arrVals(ev.stock.annual.annualRev).slice(0,5).reverse();` in evaluateAll(), then buildSparkline reads `ev._sparkVals`.

findings.category: O(n²) — full eligible set re-filtered per mode per main()
findings.title: eligibleForMode and topAllMust called twice per mode in summary loop
findings.details: After buildHtml() runs (which already calls eligibleForMode per mode at line 573-574), main() at line 1259-1263 re-runs `eligibleForMode(evaluated, modeId)` and `topAllMust(eligible, modeId, args.topN)` for each mode just to print a 1-line console summary. This re-iterates all ~4000 evaluated stocks × 3 modes again — a redundant ~12k filter operations.
findings.impact: Wasted ~few seconds of CPU per CI run. Plus topAllMust internally calls Runner.METHODS.find (F-PF-003) per mode.
findings.fix: Capture the `eligibleByMode` map already computed in buildHtml() (line 573-574) and pass it back to main(); or precompute eligibleByMode + topAllMust once and consume in both buildHtml and main.

findings.category: Sync sortByStaleness opens every snapshot file with three syscalls
findings.title: sortByStaleness does fs.openSync + readSync + closeSync per ticker just to extract asOf
findings.details: sortByStaleness runs once at start of pullAll, but it serially does 4000 × (existsSync + openSync + readSync + closeSync) = 16k blocking syscalls before any network I/O starts. With ~1ms per syscall on Windows/CI, that's 4-15s of pure I/O wait before the first Yahoo call.
findings.impact: Adds ~5-15s startup latency to every pull. On a CI run that's bounded by Yahoo, this is dominant only at startup.
findings.fix: (a) Parallelize via Promise.all over fs.promises.open in batches; (b) Skip the optimization entirely if FUNDAMENTALS_MAX_AGE_DAYS is large (the staleness sort is only valuable when most snapshots are stale, which they usually aren't); (c) Cache asOf in _manifest-full.json — manifest is written at end of run and contains ticker → status, so reading manifest.results once and using the manifest's pulled_at as proxy is one read instead of 4000.

findings.category: Memory growth across long-running detect-changes
findings.title: Deep-clone of full methodState + methodHistory at start, retained until save
findings.details: Line 218-221 does `JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(state.methodState))` and same for methodHistory. For 4000 tickers × 25 methods × ~10-week history entries each, methodHistory can be ~10-20MB. Deep-cloning this doubles peak RAM. Then per-stock iteration (line 232-258) builds `newHist[methodId] = Trend.appendHistory(...)` which adds more allocations.
findings.impact: RAM doubles transiently for methodHistory. ~10-40MB transient.
findings.fix: Don't deep-clone — mutate in place since the script writes the file at the end and process exits. Alternatively use structuredClone() (faster than JSON parse/stringify) if old-state preservation is needed.

findings.category: FX rate lookup table not in hash map shape
findings.title: REGION_BY_CURRENCY / FX_TO_USD are plain objects — fine, but normalizeRegion does regex chain per ticker
findings.details: normalizeRegion (line 138-155) runs up to 9 separate regex tests per ticker after the currency hash lookup misses. 4000 tickers × ~3 regex tests avg = 12k regex evaluations. Plus regexes are recompiled each call (literals inside function body).
findings.impact: Minor.
findings.fix: Hoist regexes to module scope; convert to a single composed regex with named groups; or pre-build a {prefix → region} table.

findings.category: Manifest write race / per-100 syscall in worker loop
findings.title: Incremental manifest write inside worker pool — 40 writes per 4000-ticker pull
findings.details: Every 100 tickers, writeManifestIncremental writes _manifest.json via fs.writeFileSync. For 4000 tickers that's 40 manifest writes — each a full O(n) stringify of the slim object. With worker pool, this is also potentially called concurrently, and the boolean mutex (line 611) prevents collisions but loses writes (skipped writes between two concurrent attempts). Each write is also a blocking syscall in the worker.
findings.impact: 40 × (stringify + sync write) = ~few hundred ms total. Mutex-skip can lose intermediate manifest checkpoints.
findings.fix: Use fs.promises.writeFile + atomic rename via promise-pool; or write incremental manifest from a single dedicated background timer (setInterval) instead of from worker threads. Or simply remove the incremental write — _manifest-full.json is written once at the end.

findings.category: Repeated full-array iteration to dedupe by company
findings.title: dedupeByCompany compiles regex once but still does normalize per-stock and Map insert
findings.details: Good news: COMPANY_SUFFIX_REGEX is hoisted (F-PF-011 fix already in place, line 156-157). However, the function still does for each stock: lowercase + diacritic replace + regex replace + non-alnum strip = 5 string operations per stock. For 4000 stocks that's 20k string ops. Map-based deduplication is O(1) per insert, so the total is O(n) — acceptable. Mention only because the regex replace is the most expensive (regex with /g + 12 alternation branches).
findings.impact: Minor — ~few hundred ms.
findings.fix: If profile shows hot, precompute norm(name) once during loadStocks rather than at dedupe time. Lower priority.

top_impact: F-PF-006 — five pipeline stages each re-parse the snapshot universe independently (largest aggregate cost)
F-PF-001 — Runner.getMethods() called per stock recreates array 4000+ times
F-PF-004 — Three scripts still do serial fs.readFileSync over the universe; snapshot-methods-history already shows the async pattern
F-PF-009 — async batch loader uses sync readFileSync, defeats Promise.all parallelism
F-PF-002 — method summary uses repeated rows.filter() calls; double-scan over ~100k tuples per method run
F-PF-017 — eligibleForMode + topAllMust run twice per mode (in buildHtml and in main summary)
quick_wins: Cache Runner.getMethods() / Runner.METHODS in a Map keyed by id at module scope
Replace fs.readFileSync with fs.promises.readFile in snapshot-methods-history.js loadFilesAsync
Drop indent:2 from pull-yahoo.js snapshot writes
Hoist regex literals in pull-yahoo.js processOne/catch block to module scope
Single-pass tally for method summary counts in generate-methods-report.js
summary.architectural_recommendation: Move to a single in-memory snapshot store loaded once after pull, then pipe detect-changes / generate-methods-report / generate-modes-report / snapshot-methods-history / sector-medians-compute against the shared store within one Node process. Largest single performance win available.
